interest: Behind this cluster is a broad coalition interest — parents, equity advocates, employers, unions, researchers, and government reformers — in a reform that actually reduces the SES-skewed test-prep arms race rather than merely relabelling the same selection machinery.
value: The tension is equity (expanding access for able low-SES/Roma pupils) versus reliability/procedural integrity (not deploying an unvalidated SES proxy or unproven coaching-resistant assessment that could reproduce or legitimise the existing skew).
fear: The shared fear is a theatrical reform that swaps one opaque selection mechanism for another — informal selection re-emerging via interviews and discretionary criteria while the reform claims to have solved the problem — leaving children under the same pressure and inequality under a new label.
affected: low-SES and Roma pupils and their families
teachers in selective and general schools
assumption: The claim rests on the assumption that a gaming-resistant, validated SES indicator and a demonstrably less-coachable assessment can be constructed, and that a pilot can reliably detect informal-selection substitution before nationwide rollout.
empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: no validated SES indicator currently exists in Hungarian administrative data, the causal claim that a redesigned exam changes who applies rests on disputed evidence (Kertesi & Kezdi vs. Horn), and coaching-resistance has not been validated at national scale.
